## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: Before using any chrome browser tools, you MUST first load them using ToolSearch.**

Chrome browser tools are MCP tools that require loading before use. Before calling any mcp__claude-in-chrome__* tool:
1. Use ToolSearch with `select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__<tool_name>` to load the specific tool
2. Then call the tool

For example, to get tab context:
1. First: ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp"
2. Then: Call mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena

content.content: 1 # AI Usages Tracker 2 3 AI Usages Tracker is a macOS menu bar app for monitoring AI assistant usage limits and account status. It keeps current usage visible at a glance, highlights the active local account, records usage history, and surfaces vendor status issues that may affect usage fetching. 4 5 The project aims to provide: 6 7 - Menu bar usage tracking for supported assistants. 8 - Active-account monitoring from each assistant's local configuration. 9 - Configurable usage history charts for recent consumption trends. 10 - Vendor status and outage visibility in the app popover. 11 - Configurable refresh behavior for automatic polling. 12 13 ## Installation 14 15 Install with Homebrew: 16 17 ```sh 18 brew tap fcamblor/tap 19 brew install --cask ai-usages-tracker 20 ``` 21 22 ## First Launch 23 24 On a fresh install, the menu bar shows a **Configure AI Metrics** call to action — no segments are pre-seeded so the app does not assume which assistant or metrics matter to you. 25 26 27 28 Click the menu bar item to open the popover, then **Open Settings** to add your first segment (vendor, account, metric, display options). Once at least one segment is configured, the menu bar label switches to live usage data. 29 30 ## Screenshots & Features 31 32 ### Multi-account support 33 34 Monitor multiple AI assistant accounts side by side. The active account is highlighted so the currently billed usage is always visible at a glance. 35 36 37 38 ### Color-coded usage gauges 39 40 Each usage bar changes color as consumption approaches the limit, making it easy to spot which metric needs attention without reading numbers. 41 42 43 44 ### Customizable menu bar display 45 46 Configure which segments appear in the menu bar label — vendor icon, colored status dot, metric short label, usage percentage, and time until reset — and choose the separator between them. Each segment has its own display options: the usage percentage can show either consumed or remaining value, and the time-until-reset label can hide the minutes component when the remaining time exceeds one day. 47 48 49 50 ### Vendor status alerts 51 52 When an assistant vendor reports an incident, a status banner appears at the top of the popover so usage anomalies can be attributed to known outages. 53 54 55 56 ### Configurable chart panels 57 58 Configure charts from **Settings > Charts**: keep the default all-metrics panel, add multiple panels, or build custom series that target a vendor, the currently active account or a specific account, a metric, a color, and a line style. 59 60 61 62 ### Usage history charts 63 64 Switch to the chart view to see consumption trends over the past 6 hours, 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days, or the full recorded history. 65 66 Hovering a chart shows the nearest values for each visible series. When a time-window metric expires before the next upstream reading, the history records that value as null so the chart breaks the line instead of drawing a misleading bridge across the reset. 67 68 69 70 ## How It Works 71 72 ### Non-intrusive by design 73 74 AI Usages Tracker never writes to, modifies, or migrates any AI assistant configuration. It reads credentials from the same local files and Keychain entries that each assistant already maintains; the assistant itself is unaware the tracker is running. Uninstalling the app leaves every assistant configuration exactly as it was. 75 76 ### Account auto-discovery 77 78 No manual account setup is required. While the app runs, it periodically scans the credential sources defined for each supported vendor. Any account it finds is automatically surfaced in the popover and becomes available for menu bar segment configuration. Removing a credential from the source makes the account disappear on the next refresh. 79 80 ### Multiple accounts per vendor 81 82 The tracker can display multiple accounts for the same vendor simultaneously. For vendors where only one account can be active at a time — such as Claude Code — the app highlights the active account so the currently billed usage is always clear at a glance. 83 84 ### Snapshot-based history 85 86 Usage data is never streamed live. The app takes periodic snapshots of the current usage figures and appends each changed snapshot to a daily log. Charts are rendered from that append-only history, so they reflect the shape of consumption over time rather than a single instant. Expired time-window metrics are preserved as null points, which lets charts show gaps when a metric is temporarily unavailable or has crossed a reset boundary. 87 88 ### Configurable refresh interval 89 90 The polling frequency is adjustable in the app preferences. A shorter interval gives more granular snapshots at the cost of more API calls; a longer interval reduces traffic at the cost of coarser history. The value applies immediately without restarting the app. 91 92 ### Menu bar segments from discovered accounts 93 94 Each entry that appears in the menu bar label is a configurable segment drawn from the auto-discovered accounts. The segment list is initialized on first launch and can be reordered or trimmed in the preferences panel at any time. 95 96 ### Configurable chart panels 97 98 The history tab is driven by the chart list in **Settings > Charts**. A fresh install or existing profile without chart preferences is seeded with one "All available metrics" chart. Users can rename, reorder, delete, or add chart panels; custom panels can pin individual series to the currently active account or to a specific account. 99 100 ## Supported Assistants 101 102 Each assistant entry below corresponds to a vendor-specific connector and a dated API snapshot under `docs/vendors/`. Adding or evolving a vendor goes through the workflow described in [`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`](docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md): a GitHub issue, a tester DMG built by CI, ≥ 2 distinct tester confirmations, and finally a tagged release that re-attaches the new entry to this section. 103 104 ### Claude Code 105 106 AI Usages Tracker reads the active Claude Code account from `~/.claude.json` and retrieves OAuth credentials from the macOS Keychain service `Claude Code-credentials`. 107 108 ### Codex 109 110 AI Usages Tracker reads Codex auth from the first available source: 111 112 - `CODEX_HOME/auth.json` 113 - `~/.config/codex/auth.json` 114 - `~/.codex/auth.json` 115 - macOS Keychain service `Codex Auth` 116 117 ### GitHub Copilot CLI 118 119 AI Usages Tracker reads GitHub Copilot auth from the first available source: 120 121 - `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable 122 - macOS Keychain entry `gh:github.com` (written by the `gh` CLI) 123 - `gh` `hosts.yml`, searched as `$GH_CONFIG_DIR/hosts.yml`, then `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/gh/hosts.yml`, then `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` 124 125 The active GitHub login is read from `hosts.yml` (`github.com.user`). 126 127 ## Data Locations 128 129 AI Usages Tracker keeps its runtime data under the current user's home directory. 130 131 ### Cache and Logs 132 133 The app-owned cache directory is: 134 135 ```text 136 ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/ 137 ``` 138 139 It contains: 140 141 - `usages.json`: latest usage snapshot used by the menu bar UI. 142 - `usages.json.lock`: advisory lock file used to coordinate safe reads and writes. 143 - `usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`: append-only daily usage-history snapshots, grouped by UTC date. 144 - `app.log`: app lifecycle, polling, persistence, and UI-adjacent logs. 145 - `claude-usages-connector.log`: Claude Code connector and status-fetch logs. 146 - `codex-usages-connector.log`: Codex connector and status-fetch logs. 147 - `copilot-usages-connector.log`: GitHub Copilot CLI connector logs. 148 - `*.log.1`: rotated log backups. Each managed log rotates after 5 MB. 149 150 Deleting the cache directory resets local usage snapshots, history, and logs. It does not remove assistant credentials or macOS user preferences. 151 152 ### User Preferences 153 154 Preferences are stored with macOS `UserDefaults.standard`. For the bundled app, the expected preferences file is: 155 156 ```text 157 ~/Library/Preferences/io.github.fcamblor.ai-usages-tracker.plist 158 ``` 159 160 The app stores these preference keys: 161 162 - `ai-tracker.refreshIntervalSeconds`: automatic refresh interval. 163 - `ai-tracker.launchAtLogin`: launch-at-login preference mirrored from the system login item state. 164 - `ai-tracker.logLevel`: selected log verbosity. 165 - `ai-tracker.menuBarSegments`: configured menu bar label segments. 166 - `ai-tracker.menuBarSegmentsInitialized`: internal flag that prevents reseeding segments after the user edits them. 167 - `ai-tracker.menuBarSeparator`: separator inserted between menu bar label segments. 168 - `ai-tracker.chartConfigurations`: configured history chart panels and custom series. 169 - `ai-tracker.chartConfigurationsInitialized`: internal flag that prevents reseeding charts after the user edits them. 170 171 Assistant OAuth credentials are not stored in this preferences file. They are read from the assistant-specific files and Keychain services listed above. 172 173 ## Color Display 174 175 Colors are used as status signals, not as persisted data. They are computed at render time from usage metrics, vendor branding, or incident severity. 176 177 ### Usage Severity Colors 178 179 Time-window metrics are compared against their theoretical pace for the current window: 180 181 ```text 182 consumption ratio = actual usage percent / expected usage percent at this point in the window 183 ``` 184 185 That ratio drives the colored status dot in the menu bar and the gauge fill color in the popover: 186 187 | Tier | Ratio | Color | 188 | --- | ---: | --- | 189 | Comfortable | `< 0.7` | system green | 190 | On track | `0.7 - 0.9` | system blue | 191 | Approaching | `0.9 - 1.0` | system yellow | 192 | Over pace | `1.0 - 1.2` | system orange | 193 | Critical | `1.2 - 1.6` | system red | 194 | Exhausted | `>= 1.6` | purple `#7B2FBE` | 195 196 Pay-as-you-go metrics, such as Codex credits, render as plain text without a severity dot. 197 198 ## Build From Source 199 200 Requirements: 201 202 - macOS 14 or later. 203 - Swift 6 toolchain or Xcode command line tools. 204 - Homebrew, if installing the distributed cask. 205 206 Build and launch the app bundle from the repository root: 207 208 ```sh 209 ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh 210 open "dist/AI Usages Tracker.app" 211 ``` 212 213 ## Development 214 215 Run development commands from `AIUsagesTrackers/`, the Swift package root: 216 217 ```sh 218 swift build 219 swift run AIUsagesTrackers 220 swift test 221 swift package clean 222 ``` 223 224 Additional project notes: 225 226 - [Development guide](docs/DEVELOPMENT.md) 227 - [Architecture overview](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) 228

file.content: # AI Usages Tracker AI Usages Tracker is a macOS menu bar app for monitoring AI assistant usage limits and account status. It keeps current usage visible at a glance, highlights the active local account, records usage history, and surfaces vendor status issues that may affect usage fetching. The project aims to provide: - Menu bar usage tracking for supported assistants. - Active-account monitoring from each assistant's local configuration. - Configurable usage history charts for recent consumption trends. - Vendor status and outage visibility in the app popover. - Configurable refresh behavior for automatic polling. ## Installation Install with Homebrew: ```sh brew tap fcamblor/tap brew install --cask ai-usages-tracker ``` ## First Launch On a fresh install, the menu bar shows a **Configure AI Metrics** call to action — no segments are pre-seeded so the app does not assume which assistant or metrics matter to you. Click the menu bar item to open the popover, then **Open Settings** to add your first segment (vendor, account, metric, display options). Once at least one segment is configured, the menu bar label switches to live usage data. ## Screenshots & Features ### Multi-account support Monitor multiple AI assistant accounts side by side. The active account is highlighted so the currently billed usage is always visible at a glance. ### Color-coded usage gauges Each usage bar changes color as consumption approaches the limit, making it easy to spot which metric needs attention without reading numbers. ### Customizable menu bar display Configure which segments appear in the menu bar label — vendor icon, colored status dot, metric short label, usage percentage, and time until reset — and choose the separator between them. Each segment has its own display options: the usage percentage can show either consumed or remaining value, and the time-until-reset label can hide the minutes component when the remaining time exceeds one day. ### Vendor status alerts When an assistant vendor reports an incident, a status banner appears at the top of the popover so usage anomalies can be attributed to known outages. ### Configurable chart panels Configure charts from **Settings > Charts**: keep the default all-metrics panel, add multiple panels, or build custom series that target a vendor, the currently active account or a specific account, a metric, a color, and a line style. ### Usage history charts Switch to the chart view to see consumption trends over the past 6 hours, 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days, or the full recorded history. Hovering a chart shows the nearest values for each visible series. When a time-window metric expires before the next upstream reading, the history records that value as null so the chart breaks the line instead of drawing a misleading bridge across the reset. ## How It Works ### Non-intrusive by design AI Usages Tracker never writes to, modifies, or migrates any AI assistant configuration. It reads credentials from the same local files and Keychain entries that each assistant already maintains; the assistant itself is unaware the tracker is running. Uninstalling the app leaves every assistant configuration exactly as it was. ### Account auto-discovery No manual account setup is required. While the app runs, it periodically scans the credential sources defined for each supported vendor. Any account it finds is automatically surfaced in the popover and becomes available for menu bar segment configuration. Removing a credential from the source makes the account disappear on the next refresh. ### Multiple accounts per vendor The tracker can display multiple accounts for the same vendor simultaneously. For vendors where only one account can be active at a time — such as Claude Code — the app highlights the active account so the currently billed usage is always clear at a glance. ### Snapshot-based history Usage data is never streamed live. The app takes periodic snapshots of the current usage figures and appends each changed snapshot to a daily log. Charts are rendered from that append-only history, so they reflect the shape of consumption over time rather than a single instant. Expired time-window metrics are preserved as null points, which lets charts show gaps when a metric is temporarily unavailable or has crossed a reset boundary. ### Configurable refresh interval The polling frequency is adjustable in the app preferences. A shorter interval gives more granular snapshots at the cost of more API calls; a longer interval reduces traffic at the cost of coarser history. The value applies immediately without restarting the app. ### Menu bar segments from discovered accounts Each entry that appears in the menu bar label is a configurable segment drawn from the auto-discovered accounts. The segment list is initialized on first launch and can be reordered or trimmed in the preferences panel at any time. ### Configurable chart panels The history tab is driven by the chart list in **Settings > Charts**. A fresh install or existing profile without chart preferences is seeded with one "All available metrics" chart. Users can rename, reorder, delete, or add chart panels; custom panels can pin individual series to the currently active account or to a specific account. ## Supported Assistants Each assistant entry below corresponds to a vendor-specific connector and a dated API snapshot under `docs/vendors/`. Adding or evolving a vendor goes through the workflow described in [`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`](docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md): a GitHub issue, a tester DMG built by CI, ≥ 2 distinct tester confirmations, and finally a tagged release that re-attaches the new entry to this section. ### Claude Code AI Usages Tracker reads the active Claude Code account from `~/.claude.json` and retrieves OAuth credentials from the macOS Keychain service `Claude Code-credentials`. ### Codex AI Usages Tracker reads Codex auth from the first available source: - `CODEX_HOME/auth.json` - `~/.config/codex/auth.json` - `~/.codex/auth.json` - macOS Keychain service `Codex Auth` ### GitHub Copilot CLI AI Usages Tracker reads GitHub Copilot auth from the first available source: - `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable - macOS Keychain entry `gh:github.com` (written by the `gh` CLI) - `gh` `hosts.yml`, searched as `$GH_CONFIG_DIR/hosts.yml`, then `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/gh/hosts.yml`, then `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml` The active GitHub login is read from `hosts.yml` (`github.com.user`). ## Data Locations AI Usages Tracker keeps its runtime data under the current user's home directory. ### Cache and Logs The app-owned cache directory is: ```text ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/ ``` It contains: - `usages.json`: latest usage snapshot used by the menu bar UI. - `usages.json.lock`: advisory lock file used to coordinate safe reads and writes. - `usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`: append-only daily usage-history snapshots, grouped by UTC date. - `app.log`: app lifecycle, polling, persistence, and UI-adjacent logs. - `claude-usages-connector.log`: Claude Code connector and status-fetch logs. - `codex-usages-connector.log`: Codex connector and status-fetch logs. - `copilot-usages-connector.log`: GitHub Copilot CLI connector logs. - `*.log.1`: rotated log backups. Each managed log rotates after 5 MB. Deleting the cache directory resets local usage snapshots, history, and logs. # Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

## Menu Bar Space and the Notch

On MacBooks with a notch, the usable menu bar width is split between the left side (occupied by app menus of the focused application) and the right side (occupied by status items). When the focused app exposes many menus, status items — including this tracker's label — can be hidden behind the notch, especially once several configurable segments are enabled.

This is a system-wide constraint rather than something the app can work around on its own. A robust workaround is to use [Ice](https://icemenubar.app/) by Jordan Baird, a free menu bar manager that hides, groups, and arranges status items so the tracker stays visible regardless of the focused app's menu width:

```sh
brew install --cask jordanbaird-ice
```

On macOS Tahoe, prefer the beta cask, which tracks the fixes required for that release:

```sh
brew install --cask jordanbaird-ice@beta
```

## Screenshots & Features

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

46c3c02 fix(menubar): stop status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU (#39)
* fix(menubar): break status-item render feedback loop pegging CPU

Setting `button.image = ...` with a non-template NSImage causes AppKit
to recompose the NSStatusItem replicant view, which flips
`button.effectiveAppearance` — re-firing the KVO observer that just
triggered the render. The loop pegged the main thread at ~600
renders/s (sampled at 96% CPU), entirely inside
`CA::Transaction::commit` → `NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView drawRect`
→ `NSCreateStatusItemShadowImage`.

Two redundant dedup keys break the loop:
- `lastRenderedKey` short-circuits `refreshStatusItemImage()` when the
  combination of segments, text, separator, resolved isDark and
  isUnconfigured matches the previous render — so the no-op assignment
  that propagates back through KVO never happens.
- `lastResolvedIsDark` filters appearance KVO fires so we only re-render
  when the dark/aqua resolution actually changes, not on every spurious
  AppKit-internal appearance churn.

Measured impact (idle, popover closed): 96% → 0.0% CPU; appearance KVO
short-circuit ratio at startup observed at 16/17 fires.

Entire-Checkpoint: 910295e4d170

* test(menubar): extract MenuBarRefreshController and cover dedup logic

The two dedup locks that broke the render feedback loop were inlined in
AppDelegate, where the AppKit dependencies (NSStatusItem, NSImage,
KVO on effectiveAppearance) make them awkward to test. Extract the
pure dedup state into `MenuBarRefreshController` in the lib target,
so the cache invariants can be asserted without standing up AppKit.

The controller exposes:
- `refresh(key:)` — invokes the render callback iff `key` differs from
  the last accepted render; the call that propagates back through KVO
  becomes a no-op and the feedback loop terminates.
- `shouldHandleAppearanceChange(isDark:)` — lets the appearance KVO
  observer filter fires that do not move the resolved dark/aqua flag.
- `renderCount` — exposed for the upcoming integration smoke test.

Tests cover the contract that prevents the regression:
- First refresh always renders exactly once.
- 1000 identical refreshes coalesce to one render.
- Each of the five MenuBarRenderKey fields independently invalidates
  the cache (one test per field, so a future field addition that
  forgets to participate in equality fails locally).
- Appearance fires with unchanged isDark are reported as no-op;
  actual flips are authorised then re-filtered.

Entire-Checkpoint: 1d1a147667cd

* test(menubar): add opt-in smoke test for the render feedback loop

The dedup unit tests cover the controller's contract in isolation, but
they cannot reproduce the actual NSStatusItem ↔ effectiveAppearance KVO
feedback that caused the original CPU spike — that requires a real
AppKit process with a graphical session. This commit adds an
integration smoke test that:

- launches the compiled AIUsagesTrackers binary,
- sandboxes its cache via AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR (so the user's
  ~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker is untouched),
- reads the menu bar render count flushed every 2 s by the running app
  (new AI_TRACKER_EXPORT_RENDER_COUNT mechanism),
- asserts the count stays below 100 over an 8 s idle window.

Before the fix the same observation window yielded ~4800 renders
(600/s × 8 s). I verified the test discriminates correctly by
temporarily removing both dedup locks: count jumped to 4299, the test
failed as expected.

The test is opt-in (RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1) because it:
- spawns a real AppKit process (~10 s wall time, intrusive),
- needs a graphical session for NSStatusBar,
- may be inconsistent on headless CI runners where the
  NSStatusItemReplicantShadowView shadowing machinery doesn't fire.

The binary path is taken from AIUT_BINARY_PATH if set, otherwise from
`.build/<triple>/{debug,release}/AIUsagesTrackers` relative to cwd, so
local developers can run it with `swift build && RUN_SMOKE_TESTS=1
swift test --filter StatusItemRenderSmoke`.

Two small support changes in the lib:
- `Loggers.cacheDir` becomes public and honours AI_TRACKER_CACHE_DIR
  so the test can divert all on-disk state to a temp dir.
- `AppPidGuard` is initialised from `Loggers.cacheDir` instead of
  recomputing the path from `$HOME`, so the sandbox applies to the
  pid file too.

Entire-Checkpoint: 77d5c88e0de8
---
447aad8 fix(menubar): use single source of truth for dot color when percent unknown (#38)
When a time-window metric's percent is unknown (resetAt missing/elapsed,
thus tier == nil), the dot color previously fell back to the surrounding
text color. This caused inconsistency between the real menu bar and the
Settings preview when they differed in appearance (e.g. dark menu bar vs
light Settings window).

Solution: add Optional<ConsumptionTier>.dotNSColor as the single source of
truth. Unknown case maps to a fixed mid-gray (not appearance-adaptive),
ensuring consistent visuals across surfaces. Add a contrasted stroke around
the dot (text color) for visibility against menu-bar wallpapers that happen
to match the fill color.

The fill is now data-driven (segment-identity); the stroke is context-driven
(for legibility only).

All tests pass.


Entire-Checkpoint: bf677a6168ea

Co-authored-by: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

docs(readme): document menu bar space constraints on notch-equipped MacBooks

docs(readme): add menu bar space and notch section with Ice workaround
```

docs(readme): document menu bar space contention with notch workaround
```
